inference_unit.problem: The paper addresses the long-standing, practically important but theoretically unresolved problem of how to treat Coulomb repulsion in microscopic, first-principles theories of phonon-mediated superconductivity so as to obtain controlled, quantitative predictions of superconducting transition temperatures. Physical context: conventional (phonon-mediated) superconductors are described at low energy by a Fermi-surface theory in which phonon-mediated attraction competes with electron-electron (Coulomb) repulsion; predicting the superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ requires reliable low-energy (Cooper-channel) effective interactions. Prior approaches and limitations: (i) Migdal–Eliashberg (ME) theory provides the basic low-energy framework but as commonly implemented relies on a semi-phenomenological "downfolding" in which the full, long-range, dynamical Coulomb interaction is replaced by a single phenomenological static pseudopotential $\mu^{*}$ (often taken as an empirical number $0.1\text{–}0.2$); (ii) Random Phase Approximation (RPA) treatments of dynamical screening neglect vertex corrections and other beyond-RPA many-body effects, producing internally inconsistent and sometimes physically implausible predictions (e.g., dynamical-RPA predictions of attractive effective Coulomb interaction at moderate interaction strengths); (iii) Density Functional Perturbation Theory (DFPT) computes an effective electron-phonon coupling $\lambda$ from Kohn–Sham response but replaces many-body electron correlations by an exchange–correlation potential, leaving uncertain how accurately $\lambda$ corresponds to the quasiparticle coupling entering the true low-energy pairing problem for correlated electrons; (iv) brute-force solutions of the full Bethe–Salpeter equation (BSE) with full momentum-frequency dependence are computationally prohibitive for realistic materials and are generally implemented with uncontrolled approximations (e.g., RPA screening); (v) numerical diagrammatic and quantum Monte Carlo methods had not previously produced controlled, high-precision values of the Cooper-channel quasiparticle scattering amplitude and therefore of a microscopic $\mu^{*}$ across the density range relevant to simple metals. Scientific consequences of these gaps: without a controlled microscopic definition and computation of $\mu^{*}$ and without a validated mapping between DFPT-computed electron–phonon matrix elements and the quasiparticle e–ph coupling entering the ME/BSE equations, first-principles $T_{c}$ predictions for many simple and weakly correlated metals retain order-of-magnitude uncertainties (especially for sub-Kelvin $T_{c}$), and conceptual questions remain about when dynamical screening or vertex corrections can change the sign or magnitude of the effective pairing interaction. This combination of theoretical deficiencies prevents reliable ab initio prediction of low $T_{c}$ values, of pressure-induced suppression of superconductivity, and of the regimes where purely electronic pairing (plasmon-mediated) might occur.

conclusion.title: Controlled low-energy downfolding and precise ME/BSE formulation

conclusions.conclusion:
A controlled effective-field-theory downfolding of the particle-particle Bethe–Salpeter equation (BSE) to a frequency-only, Fermi-surface theory is established, yielding precise microscopic definitions of the Coulomb pseudopotential and the effective electron–phonon coupling and exact forms of the low-energy Cooper-channel equations up to specified small parameters. Concretely, the downfolded anomalous-vertex BSE for the Fermi-surface-averaged anomalous vertex $\Lambda_{\omega}$ is $$ \Lambda_{\omega} = \eta_{\omega} + \pi T \sum_{|\omega^{\prime}|<\omega_{c}} \big(\lambda_{\omega\omega^{\prime}} - \mu_{\omega_{c}}\big)\frac{z_{\omega^{\prime}}^{\mathrm{ph}}}{|\omega^{\prime}|}\,\Lambda_{\omega^{\prime}} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}},\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}},\frac{T}{\omega_{c}}\right), $$ and the critical-gap (linearized ME) equation at $T_{c}$ is $$ \Delta_{\omega} = \pi T_{c}\sum_{|\omega^{\prime}|<\omega_{c}} \big(\lambda_{\omega\omega^{\prime}} - \mu^{*}\big)\frac{z_{\omega^{\prime}}^{\mathrm{ph}}}{|\omega^{\prime}|}\,\Delta_{\omega^{\prime}} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}},\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}\right), $$ where $\lambda_{\omega\omega'}$ is the Fermi-surface-averaged phonon-mediated interaction, $z_{\omega}^{\mathrm{ph}}$ is the frequency-dependent quasiparticle weight due to electron–phonon coupling, and $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ is the coherent, particle–particle-irreducible effective electron–electron interaction defined with respect to the low-energy cutoff $\omega_{c}$; the Bogoliubov–Tolmachev–Shirkov renormalization relation $$ \mu_{\omega_{c}}=\frac{\mu_{\omega_{c}'} }{1+\mu_{\omega_{c}'}\ln\!\frac{\omega_{c}'}{\omega_{c}}} $$ connects values at different cutoff scales. The derivation is controlled provided the small parameters $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}}$, $\omega_{c}^{2}/\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}$, and $T/\omega_{c}$ are small; in particular it requires a gapped plasmon ($\omega_{\mathrm{p}}\gg \omega_{c}$) and a separation $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$.
Physical/scientific context: predicting superconducting instabilities in metals requires a low-energy theory for quasiparticles near the Fermi surface where phonon-mediated attraction and Coulomb repulsion compete. Prior state: conventional practice invoked Migdal–Eliashberg equations combined with a phenomenological pseudopotential $\mu^{*}$ and DFPT-derived electron–phonon couplings but did not supply a controlled microscopic mapping from the full many-electron vertex functions to the downfolded frequency-only kernel. Specific limitations: without a rigorous downfolding one cannot unambiguously (a) separate high- and low-energy screening contributions when forming an effective low-energy kernel, (b) define $\mu^{*}$ in terms of calculable vertex functions, or (c) account for vertex and quasiparticle-weight effects on the electron–phonon coupling. Scientific consequences: these ambiguities produce large uncertainties in $T_{c}$ predictions (especially for very low $T_{c}$), obscure when dynamical screening or vertex corrections qualitatively alter pairing, and prevent a systematic extension of ab initio workflows to correlated materials. The presented derivation addresses these shortcomings by providing the explicit, cutoff-dependent relations and error estimates that justify a frequency-only BSE under well-specified conditions.

conclusion.title: Quantitative validation of the Fermi-surface downfolding

conclusions.conclusion:
The downfolded, frequency-only BSE is quantitatively accurate for conventional metallic parameters: in a representative toy model (RPA-screened electron irreducible vertex plus a model phonon interaction at $r_{s}=1.91916$ and $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}}=0.005$) the superconducting critical temperature obtained from the full frequency- and momentum-dependent BSE, $T_{c}^{(\mathrm{full})}$, and from the downfolded frequency-only BSE, $T_{c}^{(\mathrm{approx})}$, differ by $\sim 0.2\%$ (numerical values reported in the comparison).
Physical context: the theoretical downfolding relies on neglecting cross terms that mix high-energy incoherent pair propagators with low-energy coherent ones and on the assumption that plasmon dynamics do not couple strongly into the low-energy Cooper channel. Prior approaches: qualitative or heuristic downfoldings were used without quantifying the error introduced by neglecting such cross terms; full BSE solutions are computationally expensive and have been applied only with additional approximations (for example RPA) in limited contexts. Specific limitation motivating the test: it remained unclear whether the practical frequency-only reduction preserves critical temperatures at the accuracy required for predictive $T_{c}$ calculations for conventional metals. Scientific consequence: if downfolding were uncontrolled, all subsequent use of frequency-only kernels and phenomenological $\mu^{*}$ values would be suspect. The numerical comparison above demonstrates that, under conventional-metal conditions ($\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ and moderate $r_{s}$), the downfolding is justified to sub-percent precision for the test case, validating its use in the proposed workflow.
Fig. 5

conclusion.title: First-principles Coulomb pseudopotential µ* for the 3D uniform electron gas (numerical values)

conclusions.conclusion:
High-order variational DiagMC calculations combined with a homotopic resummation yield the dimensionless "bare" Coulomb pseudopotential at the Fermi-energy scale, $\mu_{E_{F}}$, for the three-dimensional uniform electron gas (UEG) as a function of Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{s}$. For integer $r_{s}$ values $1\le r_{s}\le 6$ the extracted $\mu_{E_{F}}$ (with quoted systematic uncertainties from the VDiagMC and resummation procedure) are: - $r_{s}=1$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.28(1)$, - $r_{s}=2$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.53(2)$, - $r_{s}=3$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.77(5)$, - $r_{s}=4$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.0(2)$, - $r_{s}=5$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.3(2)$, - $r_{s}=6$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.8(8)$. These values are significantly larger than estimates based on Morel–Anderson (static Yukawa/Thomas–Fermi) or static-RPA screening in the moderate-density regime ($r_{s}\gtrsim 1$) and disagree with dynamic-RPA predictions of negative pseudopotentials at $r_{s}\gtrsim 2$. The data provide a quantitative, density-dependent parameterization of $\mu_{E_{F}}$ usable in downfolded ME/BSE calculations for simple metals after adjusting for material band mass and electron density.
Physical/scientific context: the pseudopotential $\mu^{*}$ entering downfolded Migdal–Eliashberg equations determines how Coulomb repulsion reduces low-energy pairing strength. Prior approximations: static Yukawa (MA), static-RPA, and dynamic-RPA estimates disagree strongly at moderate $r_{s}$ and produce qualitatively different behavior (including negative effective interactions) because they omit crucial vertex corrections, self-energy renormalizations, or nonperturbative diagram classes. Specific limitation addressed: there were no controlled, high-precision, diagrammatic-Monte-Carlo-based computations of the Cooper-channel quasiparticle scattering amplitude and hence of a microscopic $\mu_{E_{F}}$ for $r_{s}>1$, leaving $\mu^{*}$ treated phenomenologically and inducing large uncertainties in $T_{c}$. Scientific consequences resolved by the conclusion: the provided $\mu_{E_{F}}(r_{s})$ values concretely quantify Coulomb repulsion in the UEG across the experimentally relevant density range and show that conventional phenomenological choices of $\mu^{*}$ substantially underestimate the bare interaction, explaining part of the historical discrepancies between ME predictions and experiments for simple metals.
Fig. 4; Table I

conclusion.title: Variational DiagMC homotopic expansion and resummation protocol for Cooper-channel quantities

conclusions.conclusion:
A practical computational protocol is developed and implemented that (i) constructs high-order series expansions of the quasiparticle scattering amplitude $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ using an optimized renormalized Yukawa expansion within Variational Diagrammatic Monte Carlo (VDiagMC), (ii) forms a homotopic mapping to a temperature-independent pseudopotential series $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$ via the algebraic relation $$ \mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)=\frac{\gamma_{T}(\xi)}{1-\gamma_{T}(\xi)\ln(\omega_{c}/T)}, $$ and (iii) applies conformal-map style resummation to obtain converged estimates of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi=1)$ and then rescales to $\mu_{E_{F}}$ using the Bogoliubov–Tolmachev–Shirkov relation. This protocol cures the low-temperature $(\ln T)^N$ growth of finite-order Cooper-ladder contributions, produces rapidly convergent estimates of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ (illustrated by partial-sum behavior versus $T$), and yields the numerical $\mu_{E_{F}}$ values reported.
Physical context: diagrammatic expansions for Cooper-channel quantities produce nested logarithmic divergences at low temperature (terms $\propto [\ln(E_{F}/T)]^{N}$ at order $N$) that hinder convergence of direct power-series extrapolation to the physical coupling. Prior approaches: direct diagrammatic Monte Carlo without specialized resummation cannot reliably extract the temperature-independent pseudopotential from finite-order expansions at low $T$. Specific limitation addressed: the need for a robust, numerically stable method to transform finite-order VDiagMC series into the temperature-independent pseudopotential $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ that is the appropriate low-energy input to ME/BSE. Scientific consequence: the homotopic/resummation protocol enables controlled, practical computation of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ (and hence $\mu_{E_{F}}$) from high-order diagrammatics, removing the principal numerical obstacle to directly obtaining $\mu^{*}$ from many-body theory.
Fig. 7; Fig. 14

conclusion.title: DFPT accurately reproduces the quasiparticle electron–phonon vertex in UEG

conclusions.conclusion:
For the uniform electron gas in the density range $1\le r_{s}\le 5$, the many-body quasiparticle electron–phonon vertex—expressed as the product of the quasiparticle residue, screened Coulomb interaction, and the three-point electronic vertex correction, $$ z^{e}\frac{v_{\mathbf{q}}}{\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}}\Gamma_{3}^{e}(\mathbf{k};\mathbf{q}), $$ is numerically well approximated by the DFPT (Kohn–Sham) screened ionic potential $$ \frac{v_{\mathbf{q}}}{1-(v_{\mathbf{q}}+f_{\mathrm{xc}})\chi_{0}^{e}(\mathbf{q})}\equiv g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q}), $$ i.e., $g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{q})\approx g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ with only weak residual $\mathbf{k}$-dependence over the Fermi-surface-relevant momentum range $|\mathbf{q}|\le 2k_{F}$. This near cancellation of interaction corrections (quasiparticle weight vs vertex renormalization and dielectric screening) justifies the use of DFPT-derived matrix elements to compute the effective e–ph coupling $\lambda$ for simple metals.
Physical/scientific context: the input phonon-mediated interaction $\lambda$ in low-energy ME/BSE equations must reflect the quasiparticle-level coupling between dressed electrons and physical phonons. Prior state: DFPT computes the response of the Kohn–Sham potential to ionic displacements and is widely used to obtain $\lambda$, but it replaces many-body electron correlations by an exchange–correlation kernel and it was unclear whether DFPT results correspond to the true quasiparticle coupling when Coulomb vertex corrections and quasiparticle weight are significant. Specific limitation: without validation one cannot reliably use DFPT-derived $\lambda$ in correlated or moderately interacting systems. Scientific consequence: the demonstrated numerical equality in UEG for $r_{s}\le 5$ shows DFPT provides an accurate proxy for the quasiparticle e–ph vertex in simple metals, removing a major source of practical uncertainty in ab initio superconductivity calculations for these systems.
Fig. 8

conclusion.title: Ab initio workflow combining many-body µ* and DFPT λ with precursory Cooper flow

conclusions.conclusion:
An end-to-end ab initio workflow is presented that (i) computes many-electron correlation inputs (quasiparticle density of states $N_{F}^{*}$, quasiparticle residue $z^{e}$, the static, Fermi-surface-averaged four-point vertex determining $\mu_{E_{F}}$, and the three-point quasiparticle vertex) using high-order diagrammatic many-body techniques (e.g., VDiagMC), (ii) computes phonon spectra and DFPT electron–phonon matrix elements $g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ to obtain $\lambda$, (iii) constructs the downfolded frequency-only BSE (EFT-ME) kernel using the microscopic definitions of $\mu^{*}$ and $\lambda$, and (iv) determines $T_{c}$ and the gap function by computing the normal-state anomalous-vertex $\Lambda_{\omega}$ and applying the precursory Cooper flow (PCF) scaling $\Lambda_{0}(T) = [1+g\ln(\omega_{\Lambda}/T)]^{-1}$ to infer $T_{c}$ from normal-state calculations. This workflow enables prediction of ultra-low $T_{c}$ and quantum phase transition points without empirical tuning of $\mu^{*}$.
Physical/scientific context: realistic prediction of $T_{c}$ in correlated materials requires consistent evaluation of both Coulomb repulsion and phonon-mediated attraction at the quasiparticle level and an efficient procedure to extract $T_{c}$ even when $T_{c}$ is extremely small. Prior approaches: conventional first-principles pipelines used DFPT plus a phenomenological $\mu^{*}$ and then solved ME equations (or applied McMillan/Allen–Dynes formulas), which yields large uncertainties for low $T_{c}$ and no controlled way to include many-body corrections to $\mu^{*}$. Specific limitations: lack of a rigorous mapping from many-electron vertex functions to $\mu^{*}$, uncertain relation between DFPT $g^{\mathrm{KS}}$ and quasiparticle $g$, and numerical difficulty of solving ME at very low $T$ or extracting exponentially small $T_{c}$. Scientific consequence: the proposed workflow combines controlled many-body computation of $\mu^{*}$ with validated DFPT $\lambda$ and the PCF extrapolation to produce predictive, parameter-free $T_{c}$ estimates, including ultra-low and pressure-dependent cases that were previously inaccessible.
Fig. 9

conclusion.title: Predictions and benchmarks for simple metals; pressure-driven suppression of superconductivity in Al

conclusions.conclusion:
Applying the above workflow to a set of simple metals (Li, Na, K, Mg, Al, Zn) with conduction-electron parameters fit to UEG, computed DFPT $\lambda$, and VDiagMC $\mu_{E_{F}}$, yields fitting-free $T_{c}$ predictions that substantially reduce prior uncertainties for sub-Kelvin superconductors and reconcile theory with experiment in benchmark cases. Representative numerical predictions (from Table II and Figs. 10–11) include: (i) Al at ambient pressure: $T_{c}^{\mathrm{EFT}}\approx 0.96\,$K (compared to experimental $1.2\,$K); (ii) Zn: $T_{c}^{\mathrm{EFT}}\approx 0.874\,$K (experimental $0.875\,$K); (iii) Li (hcp): $T_{c}^{\mathrm{EFT}}\approx 0.03\,$K (experimental reported $4\times 10^{-4}\,$K, with structural uncertainties noted); (iv) Na and Mg are predicted to have extremely small $T_{c}$ (e.g., practically zero within current experimental reach); (v) under assumed structural stability, Al is predicted to undergo a pressure-induced suppression of s-wave superconductivity and to become non-superconducting above a critical pressure $\sim 60\,$GPa (the calculated $T_{c}$ decreases continuously with pressure and falls below experimentally accessible values already at tens of GPa). These results resolve significant discrepancies for low-$T_{c}$ materials and show that some metals are proximate to a normal–superconducting critical point where the pair-field susceptibility would display quantum-critical (logarithmic) behavior.
Physical/scientific context: reliably predicting $T_{c}$ for simple metals is a sensitive test of any ab initio superconductivity framework because modest changes in $\mu^{*}$ or $\lambda$ can produce exponentially large changes in $T_{c}$. Prior approaches: commonly used phenomenological ranges of $\mu^{*}$ (e.g., $0.1\text{–}0.2$) introduce large errors and sometimes predict $T_{c}$ off by orders of magnitude; pure RPA or other uncontrolled approximations give inconsistent results. Specific limitation addressed: the need for parameter-free, many-body-grounded $T_{c}$ estimates and pressure dependence including possible suppression to zero by increased Coulomb effects. Scientific consequence: the presented predictions (including the estimated Al critical pressure $\sim 60\,$GPa and very low $T_{c}$ for Na and Mg) reconcile theory with experiment in benchmark cases and identify experimentally testable pressure- and material-dependent quantum-critical behavior.
Fig. 10; Fig. 11; Table II

conclusion.title: Identified validity limits and domain of applicability of the downfolded EFT-ME framework

conclusions.conclusion:
The downfolded EFT-ME framework and the frequency-only BSE are valid and controlled under the following conditions and are quantitatively accurate when they hold: (i) adiabaticity $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}}\ll 1$ (small ionic-to-electron mass ratio), (ii) separation of cut-off scales $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ (plasmon frequency $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ large compared to the high-energy cutoff $\omega_{c}$), and (iii) well-defined Fermi-surface quasiparticles (Landau Fermi liquid). The framework fails or requires full BSE treatment when any of these conditions break down, notably when plasmons soften ($\omega_{\mathrm{p}}\lesssim\omega_{c}$), in two-dimensional systems with gapless plasmon modes, in systems with emergent soft collective modes from strong correlations, in materials with very low conduction-electron density ($E_{F}\sim\omega_{D}$), or when Umklapp and multiband/core-electron contributions to screening are significant (e.g., many transition metals).
Physical context: the downfolded theory neglects certain cross terms and relies on scale separation between phonon, Cooper-channel low-energy, and plasmon scales. Prior assumptions: conventional ME downfolding was invoked heuristically without a systematic account of regimes where energy-scale separation fails. Specific limitation addressed: the necessity of stating where the proposed controlled downfolding is inapplicable so that users know when full-frequency-momentum BSE (or alternative nonperturbative approaches) is required. Scientific consequence: these explicit validity criteria guide the applicability of the method to real materials (e.g., simple metals are typically within the domain; low-density or strictly 2D systems require caution) and identify the physical regimes where dynamical screening or nonlocal Coulomb effects must be treated without downfolding approximations.

inference_unit.open_question: The paper explicitly identifies several unresolved scientific and technical issues requiring further work: (i) the accuracy and applicability of DFPT-derived electron–phonon coupling $\lambda$ in strongly correlated materials and complex band-structure systems (for example transition metals with multiband screening and substantial core-electron contributions) remains an open question and requires development of appropriate EFT-based benchmarking and corrections; (ii) implementing high-order diagrammatic renormalization and VDiagMC-type computations for lattice systems with complex band structure (loss of spherical symmetry, important Umklapp processes, multiple screening channels) is a major technical challenge and a necessary step to extend the present UEG-based results to those materials; (iii) two-dimensional systems and other settings with gapless or weakly gapped plasmon modes (e.g., isolated 2D electron gases without substrate screening) violate the plasmon-gap assumption used to justify decoupling of high- and low-energy sectors, so a careful treatment (potentially full BSE without downfolding) is required and remains to be developed; (iv) in regimes where the plasmon frequency softens (extremely high densities or exotic astrophysical conditions), or where strong electronic collective modes emerge, the present downfolding is no longer controlled and the correct theoretical treatment of Coulomb and electron–phonon interplay is unresolved; (v) the homotopic resummation protocol relies on an assumed analyticity of the diagrammatically generated function $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ and while an a posteriori numerical consistency check is provided, a rigorous proof of analyticity and further mathematical control of the conformal-map/resummation step remain open; (vi) quantitative extension to materials where conduction and core bands are not well separated, where Umklapp scattering is strong, or where spin-fluctuation contributions to pairing are important will require the design and benchmarking of computationally tractable many-body schemes that incorporate those effects without uncontrolled approximations.
